speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about the routines in the camp that they put you through.

speaker: interviewee
text: Life in the camp was gruesome. It was hard. It was regimented. Had to get up early in the morning. We were awoken by being chased out. In one camp, I remember there were two kapos, two foremen. Usually, the criminals that were also incarcerated, they were the foreman in the-- in the camps, and they were called the kapos. And a kapo was somebody you would be afraid of. In this particular camp, when we were woken in the morning, they would stay on both sides from the bunks when we jumped down from the bunks. We had to run to the washroom and on the way, they would beat us. For no reason at all, this was just a wake-up call. Both of them would stand with clubs on either side-- on both sides and we would have to run through. And the faster you run through, then the less beating you got. But this was the regular routine. And these were criminals who were incarcerated with us. Each-- each prisoner had an insignia. Homosexuals had an insignia, the criminals had an insignia, and we Jews had an insignia. Naturally, we were the majority. Life in camp consisted of getting up in the morning at 5 o'clock and roll call. And this took a while. Everybody had to be counted. Then we were served black coffee like mud, and we would be marching to the [GERMAN]. [GERMAN] is a plant. And that could have been five miles away-- kilometers, five kilometers, four kilometers. It was long-- long distance from the barracks where the camp was to the place of work. This [GERMAN] was a huge plant, as I said before. And on that plant, you could see prisoners of war from all-- every, probably every-- every country that was taking part in the war. I remember some Canadians, I remember French, I remember English. Of course, they were treated a little bit differently then we. We only met them at the-- at the camp, at the plant. They were housed in separate camps and we were in different camps. Of course, they were treated differently. In one particular case, I remember one winter-- one winter in 1944, we were standing and it was so bitter cold. The summer-- the winters were so harsh. Maybe they were-- they were harsher than we thought they were because we don't have any clothes, we don't have any food. We were skin and bones, naturally. And one of them slipped me a-- a block of chocolate. I-- this was just like a unbelievable event. And I-- naturally, I put it in my pocket-- I put it in my pocket and I came back to the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: In this particular camp, I was with my brother together. We were-- we were together and then we were separated and then we were together again. In this [GERMAN], we were together. And I came back to the camp and I-- he was in a different column, and I was in a different barrack. And I said, guess what? You'll never believe it. This was just unbelievable. A guy just slipped me so nobody should see. We were standing near a fire to warm ourselves up in the-- in the street. Like a whole city there, they-- they [INAUDIBLE]. Of course, we were well-watched and well-guarded, but within the working area, we were free to move. And we were debating, shall we trade it for bread, shall we trade it for cigarettes, or shall we eat the chocolate as it is? We both decided we'd eat the chocolate as it is. We were so starved. This camp, this particular camp, which was-- I spent most of the time. Every day after work coming back, you stayed on the [GERMAN]. The [GERMAN] is the plant. And the work was hard-- labor, manual work.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had to do so much. And we had kapos overseeing us, naturally, to see if we were doing. You couldn't goof. I-- I had very good-- in this particular camp for a while, I-- I had-- I was a privileged character. The foreman of this particular column, of this particular battalion, [BACKROUND NOISE] so to speak, took a liking to me, and he kept me as a shop steward. I was in the shop inside, issuing the tools, collecting different things, and seeing that everything is right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he also favored me. I told him I'm an artist. He asked me if I could draw a-- a portrait of his girlfriend. I said, sure. Why not? Anything to get some kind of favor of some kind. So he gave me a picture of a beautiful German girl, and I carried around [INAUDIBLE] respectively thinking that it was impossible for me draw any pictures at all. I didn't have the materials, I didn't have the space-- the place.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was just in it-- but anyway, one day I'm walking-- he sends me with an errand to another place. And I'm riding on a bicycle [INAUDIBLE]. Within the plant, you were free to move. Of course, we have to do some work. But I was a privileged character and the meister, the foreman, sent me off on an errand. I'm riding a bicycle, and I'm stopped by an SS man.

speaker: interviewee
text: What are you, a big shot of some kind? Where are you going? He starts searching me and he finds this picture of this girl-- beautiful blonde, big postal card picture. Who's the girl? I says, I don't know. He beats me up, slaps me in the face and says, what you mean you know? What's her name? I said, I don't know. Well, who is it? So I told him my-- I told him the story, my foreman gave it to me. He wants me to make a portrait, I'm an artist. Well, immediately he hauled me in, and I was reported and they made a report right away. And I remember the big-- big-- big guys in the camp [? picked ?] me up and they gave me a few good lashes. And they said, if you are not going to tell us the truth, you are going to get shot. They immediately suspected that I have something to do with sabotage, with espionage actually. Who is the girl? And I told them, I don't know her name, but she is my meister's girlfriend, and I'm supposed to make a portrait out of it. Well, to make the story short, the judenalteste, which is the head of the Jewish inmates, he was within this hearing. And he came over, and he slapped me.

speaker: interviewee
text: He gave me a good slap and he said, look, if you'll tell the truth, the real truth, we'll let you go this time. I said, this is the real truth. I don't know, but I'll give you the name, the [GERMAN], the name of the meister. And miraculously, miraculously they let me go. But they have shot and killed people for less [? offenses. ?] And this was a real sort of thing to-- to suspect, a suspicious thing. I escaped that one, but I'll never forget it. And he really saved me. The judenalteste, the head of the Jewish inmates, he really somehow saved me. Coming back from the [GERMAN] every day, you would be counted. And inadvertently, every once in a while, somebody will be missing, somebody would escape. And they later on ordained that if anybody escapes from a certain group, the whole group will be shot. And one time, they shot 20 people from this column. A guy was missing. This is in order to teach everybody a lesson to watch one for another. Because if you see that he is trying to escape, you better watch, otherwise you will be shot. One time they shot 20 people. And the-- standing there after a whole day's work in [GERMAN] plant and after marching back after being starved, literally starved out, we had to stay in line to be counted many times for hours at a time. When you finally were dismissed, then you were reporting standing in line to get your dinner for the day. The dinner consisted-- everybody had a bowl, a tin bowl, and you would get a-- a-- a bowl of soup, which was spinach most of the time. And the spinach was just with hot water without clean-- being cleaned, with the sand in it. And you would get a slice of bread. The loaves of bread were about this big. And the blockalteste, the elders of the block, of the barrack that we're in, it was his responsibility to make sure that each bread gets cut in 10 pieces and each prisoner got one slice. That bread was like mud, like-- like clay. It was sticky. It was very dark bread. This bread was cut in 10 slices, and there was a whole ceremony by deciding who gets which slice. So one of them had to turn around and say, prisoner number so-and-so gets this slice, prisoner so-and-so-- so that nobody gets a bigger slice than other one and a bowl of soup. One slice of bread and a bowl of soup at night was the food for the day and in the morning it was the black coffee. Life in camp was harsh. It was bad. It was-- people were dying like flies. Every day on the way back, you would see somebody being hauled away in a pushcart. They had pushcarts. People that couldn't walk broke down on the way, and they were taken into the camp, and they would put them in the infirmary. And depending on their condition, if they were bad, they were shipped to Auschwitz; if they were within mending process, they were mended up, patched up, and put back on the-- on the-- in the barrack. It was a big-- tremendous big camp. We were deloused every once in a while. In the winter time, we had to go to the barrack that had the showers naked. We were chased out naked. We had to come back after a hot shower to the barrack naked. I remember standing in one of the showers at the lousing-- delousing and my cousin I was with, he says to me, my god, look at yourself, just skin and bones. I says, you're telling me. Look at yourself. We were so emaciated. We were so skinny. We were just scared looking at one another. This was life in Blechhammer. People were dying constantly, work was hard. Some people traded their bread for cigarettes, but they went fast. Yeah. It was just impossible to-- people were stealing, one from or another. They were on their way, wherever somebody on the-- on the plant being with-- being with so many different nationalities, there was some haggling, some-- some bargaining, some dealing, wheeling and dealing going on. But there were so many-- so little food, so many people trying to grab whatever they could it was just impossible to survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: Somehow, how we survived I don't know. But worse than that, worse than the hunger pangs and all this here was the hopelessness, the treatment, the-- the degradation, dehumanization, the tortures, the anguish, the suffering. I remember one night I said to my brother, we met at night, do you think we'll ever get out of here? I had no hopes and expectations to get out of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they took away our mind. We went numb. We actually got numb, [? but yet ?] we had feelings. And then another thing, every so often they had their hangings we had to witness. This plant, this particular plant-- and I must touch on this here. This particular plant was very vital for the German war machine. And the English tried to bomb it, I mean to flatten it. They bombed it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So every so often, they bombed it. Now, we could tell like clockwork a-- a reconnaissance plane we would see in the air. And the Germans tried to shoot them down, and they would leave down these-- these aluminum foils to-- to camouflage it. And we knew exactly that a day or two later, there will be a bombardment. And as soon we saw them, we knew the next day it's gonna be a bombardment. And there sure was.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they had all kinds of smoke screens to blacken the-- the whole plant. The plant was tremendous big. And one time I-- the Jews were not allowed in the bunkers. The Germans and the prisoners of war were allowed in the bunkers, we were not. One time I was running and the plane was right-- right above me, and I was trying to beat the plane and a bomb fell not far away. And from the crater it created, I was knocked down and I was covered up to my head in-- in sand and I dug myself out. Had I been a few feet closer to the crater, I would have been dead. And later on towards the end, they did let us in in the bunkers for some reason. At first, we couldn't. These were very reinforced heavy cement, five-foot deep bunkers with these round things. And they were all over the plant for the Germans, for the guards. And one time, I was on one of them. They let us in and this bunk took a-- this took a direct hit. And I'll never forget-- never forget the trembling sensation, the fears. The-- the-- the fear and the trembling, the whole-- the whole world was trembling and the noise. Later on when we got up, we could see the chunk that that bomb that dug into this camp. We survived it. Life in the camp was horrible. It was awful. But somehow we survived until January the 18th, 1945. All at once, orders came, we are evacuated. We are put down in columns and we started marching. We left about 2,000 strong. I think half of us got lost on the way. What happened was we froze, most of them froze. At night, we were pushed down into what do you call-- barns. It-- it was all farmland that we walked through. And wherever you went through a farm, naturally we raided the farms and all of the-- I remember one time I got a hold of-- of potato peels and this was like-- like manna from heaven. And we got-- gobbled up all the garbage that we could raid on the way. But when we were pushed in through these farms, that's when we raided the farms. The next day, they would come and round us up to march and people with hide in stacks of hay or in these-- in these fields. And they will go with the bayonets and just make sure that everybody-- my uncle, my father's brother, I saw him a day before, the next day I didn't see him. Don't forget, the columns were hundreds-- hundreds of us going. So it was like for a stretch of a half a mile. Now, the German guards were on both sides, and if you fell on the snow or you couldn't walk anymore, they put you on a wagon at the very tail of the column and later on they shot them. One time I tried to pull a quickie. I tried-- a cousin of mine who is in Israel now, the two of us were marching, and we were in the front of the columns, in back. We were slowing down and-- it wasn't just a marching, it was just walking with-- with wrapped around paper sacks on our shoes. Fierce cold and-- and-- and in the middle of nowhere, on open highways, open roads. And we were trying to play dead. So we sort of backed up, and we were trying to at the end. And we laid down. But as we tried to play the thing, we saw they were shooting some people. Now, this one [? thing, ?] I'll never forget. And I-- I can't talk about it, either. A father and son were walking and the father was just on his last leg, literally he could not walk. And he fell in the snow, and he was-- he was just croaking. You could see his face, [? open mouth. ?] And the son was pulling him. He says, Papa, he says, you made it this far, we don't have too far to walk. Come on, I'll help you get up. And this-- the man is laying in the snow and the German came over with his rifle, and he hit the son over the head and he says, if you're not leaving him, you're not marching. I'll shoot you and I'll shoot him. And I watched and the son left his father dying in the snow. This is so impregnate-- imprinted and impregnated in my mind so bad that every time I talk about the scenes, I see the son walking away, leaving his father in the snow being shot by a German freezing in the snow.

speaker: interviewer
text: How can you translate that? I don't think there's any words to describe the anguish. If there's any words to describe the suffering, the-- the-- the torture, the mental torture that we went through on this march. This was a killer, a death march. Every day there were people lined-- their bodies were lined up on the outside to be hauled away. Now, we finally walked-- marched in into this Gross Rosen. There we was a mix of different camps, poured them in one.

speaker: interviewee
text: And from there, they put us on cattle cars, and we were taken to Buchenwald. On the way, we were bombed. From the-- we ran out, we jumped out of the cattle cars, and we were hiding underneath the cars. I'll never forget that scene, either. How we wound up in Buchenwald, there was another together, another mix of all camps from all over. And there were so many numerous camps in Poland. And people hear of Auschwitz and Buchenwald, but there were so many other killing-- I mean death factories that may not have been so well-documented and well-written. But there were camps all over the place, all over Poland. And from Buchenwald, we were segregated again, and we were shipped to Spaichingen. My brother was-- my brother, [INAUDIBLE]. He had heard-- then we were separated towards the end [INAUDIBLE]. So we were still together. And Spaichingen was absolutely a killer. [BACKROUND NOISE] There were very few people who survived there.

speaker: interviewee
text: There's another person that lived in Philadelphia, my brother and myself-- myself and this other person. How did we find out? Later on, years after the war, we were summoned to the German consulate here, and they were trying to get us to-- summoned to be witnesses for a Nazi trial. They caught somebody that was an official in-- in Spaichingen. And knowing from our documents that we were in Spaichingen, could we attest to it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were drilled at the German consulate here for two days. And after the second day, the guy that interrogated me told me, if you are interested in seeing somebody else that lives here in Philadelphia, I'll give you his telephone number. And I got in touch with him and we made contact. But as far-- so far we know, there's very few left from this particular camp. It was near the Swiss border.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the conditions in this camp that were-- you said they were very, very harsh.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was one of the harshest [BACKROUND NOISE] ones that I've been in. As I said before, good morning-- the good morning call was with two criminal inmates staying on both sides and clubbing us going to the washroom, going out in the morning to work. And the work was torture, absolutely torture. And every day, somebody tried to escape. I remember one morning, this guy was-- this inmate, he was sleeping in the bunk right next to me, and somebody stole his cap. Naturally, everybody stole from everybody else. Stole his cap so he accused the other guy of stealing the cap, and he fought with the other inmate. So later on in the day, this very same guy was trying to escape, and he was shot and then he was brought back to the camp and he was laid right in the center and everybody had to walk around him. And I says, oh my god, he just-- he just fought with the other guy this morning. He tried to escape. When they brought him, he was laying naked with bullet wounds right here. And we all had to walk around it. It wasn't enough how they-- how they tortured us in this camp. We had to look at the guy and we were-- this was a warning, if you try to do it, this is what's going to happen to you. I broke down physically in this camp, and I could not cope with it any longer. I could hardly walk. There was a name for us, Muselmann-- Muselmann, Muselmann was the name. A Muselmann was a skeleton of a person who was like a walking dead, covered with a blanket over his head, his eyes sockets suck-- sinken in, and who could hardly walk. Of course, a man like this, he was very quickly done away with. He was put on a-- put on a transport with others, and he was shipped to wherever they were shipped. I was one of them and my brother, I was picked up, and I was put on a train. And instead of-- these were the last days already and I have a document here from Dachau. I was shipped to Dachau. And not until this morning, I didn't pay attention, this was April the 13th, 1943 from Spaichingen, I was shipped to Dachau. And at Dachau, I was put in in a barrack. There were hundreds naked people in this one-- a lot of Russians, a lot of Russians prisoners-- a lot of Russian prisoners of war. The lice were eating us up alive. It was like everything was moving and everybody was covered with lice. And they left some warts, like-- not warts, open pockmarks. And this was like hell-- hell on Earth. I remember there was one window that the sun was shining and there were hundreds of prisoners like wild beasts running around. The Russians were very boisterous. There must have been new prisoners of war. And we were destined to be gassed, but for some reason or another, they put us on trains. The Americans started encircling this whole area. Dachau was not far from Munich. And they put us on trains and we were on trains for eight days. There were five long trains with thousands of prisoners. There were specific orders by Himmler, later on it was documented. There were specific orders by Himmler to ship us to Tyrol Mountains and to shoot us all because they were afraid that we would be escaping, surviving the war, and we will tell the atrocities. And I remember the life, the nights on those trains. Every morning, people were-- were-- were-- were just dropping dead. They put us 120 to 150 in this wagon, no food. And there was a lot of noise. And one time, one night the guy came in, one of the guard men, and he just hit people on the head with a rifle. And he hit me. He-- he hit my head-- my shoulder and I couldn't move my arms for a couple of days. He broke something or whatever, just to quiet the sound because the noise was unbearable and the stench. The hunger pangs, the-- the-- absolutely-- there-- there's no words to describe it. I-- I can only say one thing, people there were in it are impossible to-- to tell it, and the people who were not in it cannot possibly believe it, that those situations could have existed. Anyway, on the eighth day, we-- all of a sudden, we felt the air, we sensed it. There were rumors. Where the rumors came from, I don't know. But this was one long train with a lot of wagons. And there were several trains on these rails from all the camps to be shipped away. And we realized that we're going back and forth the same because we looked out and we saw the same landmarks. And we realized that we are not getting anyplace, we are-- and from this, people assumed that we may be trapped, we may be encircled, which was the truth. But it was hard to believe. I would never believe it, I never did believe it. And then towards the end, after about five, six days, the guards are lax. They-- they don't beat us, they opened the doors, we look out. And about on the eighth day or so, we-- I saw-- my first sight was a soldier shooting with a machine gun. [BACKROUND NOISE] I know he's not a German, I didn't know he was American. It was an American. I didn't know he was an American. And people are just running out of the wagons. I couldn't run, I could not walk. I was laying among the dead on that train. I couldn't make it out. So somebody helped me out from the wagon, and I was on the ground. And I was laying there, where everybody that could walk was running towards the liberating soldiers. And there was a little town nearby, and what they did is they robbed, they pilfered. They slaughtered every chicken and every goat and every dog that they saw on the way and they robbed the Germans. I was laying there until a Red Cross-- American Red Cross van came by. They picked me up on a stretcher, and they took me on this van. And from there, the odyssey of hospitals started.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were on the ground, you were near a town. Do you remember the name of the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. I don't remember the name of the town. But I was on the ground. It wasn't near a town. It was on the rails in the middle of nowhere. But there was a little town not far because later on, I was carried by there and I saw chaos, total chaos. A-- a town with hundreds of refugees running around, robbing houses, throwing things out on the street. And naturally, they were trying to eat and get their bodies satisfied and it was total chaos. These were the first days of the liberation. But I must tell you one thing here. There is another incident that happened to me. A day or two later, I was let go. I was-- I was regained my-- my composure and I walked. And there was lines all over. There were lines all over. And I figured, if there's a line, they must be giving something, I'll get in line. So there were lines of Germans they're getting their provisions. It was the first days of liberation, mind you. The-- the Americans-- there's no Germans-- the Americans come with all their heavy material, war material, and there's population and there's hundreds, thousands of-- of refugees, of inmates. I'm standing in line and it's muddy and it's cold. This happened May the 1st, May the 2nd. And I collapse, I black out. I fall in the mud, and I'm laying there in the line. There's a German woman standing in another line. She walks-- steps out of her line, comes over to me, picks me up, and takes me to her house and watches me and feeds me. And I'm having a-- a really high fever and whatever. I had-- later on, I had typhoid fever, and I had tuberculosis and whatever else I had. And she washes me and she calls a doctor. And I remember-- I remember I did not-- I wasn't conscious enough, but I remember he took my pulse, took my temperature, he motioned his head, and he told her to get me out. And she sat down and she said to me, I have my son somewhere in the war and the army, and I hope that somebody will pick him up and treat you-- treat him, my son, the way I treat you. She went down on the street. She picked up an American Red Cross patrol. They took me down on their hands. I remember the guy carrying me. I must have been weighing about 70 pounds, put me on a-- on a Red Cross van, took me to hospital. And that was the end of my liberation. There I was in hospitals from 1945 until-- until 19-- [PAUSES FOR  SECONDS] I'll pick up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, we'll pick up.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'll pick up later at the hospital and we'll finish that segment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. And then we can go into these other things that you want-- want to cover.

speaker: interviewee
text: What, is it an hour and a half already?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.
